title: Conditions for a t test about a mean
description: Check whether or not the the conditions have been met for a one-sample t test for a mean
topic_path: Setting up a test for a population mean
Conditions for a t test about a mean

assessment_items.question_content:
A gym advertises that its $1{,}000$ members, on average, lost $5 \text{ kg}$ in their first month of membership. A skeptical employee suspects that the actual average is lower than this, so they take an [[☃ definition 1]] of $10$ customers and look at each of their weight losses in their first month of membership.

The sample data is skewed to the right with an average weight loss of $4.25 \text{ kg}$ and a standard deviation of about $3 \text{ kg}$. The employee wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test about the mean.

**Which conditions for performing this type of significance test have been met?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "...they take an SRS of $10$ customers," and since an SRS is a simple random random sample, we can assume that the random condition is satisfied.

###The normal condition

The sample data were skewed, and the sample size is small $(n=10<30)$, so the normal condition has **not** been met.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, our sample size shouldn't exceed $10\%$ of the population size. Since the sample of $10$ members is less than $10\%$ of the population (the problem states there are $1{,}000$ customers), the independence condition has been satisfied.

###Answers

* The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
* Individual observations are independent.

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
The sampling distribution of $\bar x$ is approximately normal.

assessment_items.question_content:
Sunil and his friends have been using a group messaging app for over a year to chat with each other. He suspects that, on average, they send each other more than $100$ messages per day.

Sunil takes a random sample of $7$ days from their chat history and records how many messages were sent on those days. The sample data are strongly skewed to the right with a mean of $125$ messages and a standard deviation of $44$ messages. He wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test about the mean.

**Which conditions for performing this type of significance test have been met?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "Sunil takes a random sample of $7$ days from their chat history," so we can assume that the random condition is satisfied.

###The normal condition

The sample data were skewed, and the sample size is small $(n=7<30)$, so the normal condition has **not** been met.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, our sample size shouldn't exceed $10\%$ of the population size. Since the sample of $7$ days is less than $10\%$ of the population (the problem states that they have been using the app for over a year), the independence condition has been satisfied.

###Answers

* The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
* Individual observations are independent.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
The sampling distribution of $\bar x$ is approximately normal.

assessment_items.question_content:
Fernanda runs a large bowling league. Altogether, members of the league have played thousands of individual games over the course of the season. She suspects that the league average score is greater than $150$ per game.

Fernanda takes a random sample of $15$ scores from the league data. The scores in the sample are strongly skewed to the right with a mean of $156$ and a standard deviation of $36$. She wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test about the mean.

**Which conditions for performing this type of significance test have been met?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "...she takes a random sample of $15$ scores from the league data," so we can assume that the random condition is satisfied.

###The normal condition

The sample data were skewed, and the sample size is small $(n=15<30)$, so the normal condition has **not** been met.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, our sample size shouldn't exceed $10\%$ of the population size. Since the sample of $15$ scores is less than $10\%$ of the population (the problem states that members of the league have played thousands of individual games), the independence condition has been satisfied.

###Answers

* The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
* Individual observations are independent.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
The sampling distribution of $\bar x$ is approximately normal.

assessment_items.question_content:
Brennan is a counselor at a large high school with approximately $1{,}500$ students. He suspects that students are sleeping more than $9$ hours per night on the weekend. He takes a random sample of $12$ students and asks them how many hours they typically sleep per night on the weekend. The sample data are strongly skewed to the right with a mean of $9.25$ hours and a standard deviation of $2.8$ hours.

Brennan wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test about the mean.

**Which conditions for performing this type of significance test have been met?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "...he takes a random sample of $12$ students," so we can assume that the random condition is satisfied.

###The normal condition

The sample data were skewed, and the sample size is small $(n=12<30)$, so the normal condition has **not** been met.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, our sample size shouldn't exceed $10\%$ of the population size. Since the sample of $12$ students is less than $10\%$ of the population (the problem states that the school has approximately $1{,}500$ students), the independence condition has been satisfied.

###Answers

* The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
* Individual observations are independent.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
The sampling distribution of $\bar x$ is approximately normal.

assessment_items.question_content:
West High School is a large school with more than $2{,}000$ students. The school requires that students are assigned an average of $3.5$ hours of homework each night. The school board believes that students are being assigned less than this,  so they take a random sample of $50$ West High School students to determine if there is convincing evidence to support their suspicion. The homework times in the sample are strongly skewed to the right.

The school board wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test about the mean.

**Which conditions for performing this type of test did their sample meet?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "...they take a random sample of $50$ West High School students..," so we can assume that the random condition is satisfied.

###The normal condition

Even though the homework times in the sample are strongly skewed to the right, the normal condition has been met because of the sample size $(n=50 \geq 30)$. The central limit theorem says that the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will be approximately normal as long as our sample size is reasonably large, which it is in this case.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, our sample size shouldn't exceed $10\%$ of the population size. Since the sample of $50$ students is less than $10\%$ of the population (the problem says there are more than $2{,}000$ students at West High School), the independence condition has been satisfied.

###Answers

* The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
* The sampling distribution of $\bar x$ is approximately normal.
* Individual observations can be considered independent.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
The sampling distribution of $\bar x$ is approximately normal.
Individual observations can be considered independent.

assessment_items.question_content:
A website streams movies and television shows to millions of users. Employees know that the average time a user spends per session on their website is $2$ hours. The website changed its design, and they wanted to know if the average session length was longer than $2$ hours. They randomly sampled $100$ users and found that their session lengths had a mean of $2.75$ hours and a standard deviation of $1.55$ hours.

The employees want to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test about the mean.

**Which conditions for performing this type of test did their sample meet?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "They randomly sampled $100$ users..," so we can assume that the random condition is satisfied.

###The normal condition

Even though the problem doesn't mention if the sample data were skewed or symmetric, the normal condition has been met because of the sample size $(n=100 \geq 30)$. The central limit theorem says that the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will be approximately normal as long as our sample size is reasonably large, which it is in this case.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, our sample size shouldn't exceed $10\%$ of the population size. Since the sample of $100$ users is less than $10\%$ of the population (the problem says there are millions of users), the independence condition has been satisfied.

###Answers

* The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
* The sampling distribution of $\bar x$ is approximately normal.
* Individual observations can be considered independent.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
The sampling distribution of $\bar x$ is approximately normal.
Individual observations can be considered independent.

assessment_items.question_content:
A fast-food company advertises that the pre-cooked weight for its half-pound burgers is, on average, $0.5\text{ lbs}$. The company runs a regular quality control test that involves weighing a sample of burgers to see if the sample mean weight is significantly different than $0.5 \text{ lbs}$.

Jayda takes a random sample of $20$ burgers from a batch of over a thousand burgers. The weights in the sample are roughly symmetric with no outliers. She wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test about the mean.

**Which conditions for performing this type of test did her sample meet?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "Jayda takes a random sample of $20$ burgers from a batch..," so we can assume that the random condition is satisfied.

###The normal condition

Even though the sample is small $(n=20<30)$, the sample data are roughly symmetric with no outliers, so it satisfies the normal condition.

When our sample size is smaller than $30$, it's important to consider the shape of the sample data. If the data shows skew or outliers, we should doubt that the parent is approximately normal, and so the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ may not be normal either. But as long as the sample data looks roughly symmetric with no outliers, we can assume the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will be approximately normal as well.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, our sample size shouldn't exceed $10\%$ of the population size. Since the sample of $20$ burgers is less than $10\%$ of the population (the problem says there are over a thousand burgers in the batch), the independence condition has been satisfied.

###Answers

* The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
* The sampling distribution of $\bar x$ is approximately normal.
* Individual observations can be considered independent.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
The sampling distribution of $\bar x$ is approximately normal.
Individual observations can be considered independent.

assessment_items.question_content:
A restaurant sells over a thousand burritos every week. They advertise that these burritos weigh $250\text{ g}$. The manager took a random sample of $16$ burritos one week to test if the mean weight that week was significantly different than $250\text{ g}$. The weights of the burritos in the sample were roughly symmetric with no outliers.

The manager wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test about the mean.

**Which conditions for performing this type of test did her sample meet?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "The manager took a random sample of $16$ burritos one week..," so we can assume that the random condition is satisfied.

###The normal condition

Even though the sample is small $(n=16<30)$, the sample data are roughly symmetric with no outliers, so it satisfies the normal condition.

When our sample size is smaller than $30$, it's important to consider the shape of the sample data. If the data shows skew or outliers, we should doubt that the parent is approximately normal, and so the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ may not be normal either. But as long as the sample data looks roughly symmetric with no outliers, we can assume the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will be approximately normal as well.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, our sample size shouldn't exceed $10\%$ of the population size. Since the sample of $16$ burritos is less than $10\%$ of the population (the problem says they sell over a thousand burritos every week), the independence condition has been satisfied.

###Answers

* The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
* The sampling distribution of $\bar x$ is approximately normal.
* Individual observations can be considered independent.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
The sampling distribution of $\bar x$ is approximately normal.
Individual observations can be considered independent.

assessment_items.question_content:
Here are two different samples drawn from two different populations:

![Two dot plots show the distributions of two samples. For each sample, the number line has a scale from 5 to 15. For sample A, n = 13. The distribution is roughly symmetrical between 12 and 15, with outliers at 5 and 6. For sample B, n = 34. The distribution is left skewed between 5 and 15.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4da50e985ca72f6adfc0a332032c9d40741b0c52)

**Which sample satisfies the normal condition for performing a $t$ test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##The normal condition for a $t$ test

We need the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar x$ to be approximately normal. Since we can't see the population distribution, we make decisions based on the distribution of sample data and the sample size.

**Larger samples**

If our sample size is reasonably large $(n \geq 30)$, then the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will be approximately normal regardless of the shape of the sample data or its population (because of the central limit theorem).

**Smaller samples**

When our sample size is smaller than $30$, it's important to graph our sample data. If the data shows skew or outliers, we should doubt that the parent is approximately normal, and so the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ may not be normal either. But as long as the sample data looks roughly symmetric with no outliers, we can assume the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will be approximately normal as well.

###Examining the sample data

**Sample A**

Sample A fails the normal condition because of the small sample size and low outliers:

![The previous dot plot for sample A is displayed. n = 13. The distribution is roughly symmetrical between 12 and 15, with outliers at 5 and 6.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c169d2fc558aa364b863ec1ae7c2d26cceb0192b)

**Sample B**

Since sample B has a large enough sample size $(n=34 \geq 30)$, it passes the normal condition because of the central limit theorem. Even though the sample data doesn't look normal, the sampling distribution of sample means will be approximately normal due to the sample size.

###The answer

Only sample B satisfies the normal condition for a $t$ test.

###The answer
